seoTitle: How to Trigger a Power Automate Flow From Excel
metaDescription: Learn how to start a Power Automate flow directly from Excel using Excel Online, Office Scripts, or table changes in OneDrive and SharePoint.
intro: Excel holds a lot of the data that business workflows depend on, but manually acting on that data wastes time. This guide shows how to connect Excel to Power Automate so changes in a workbook can kick off a flow automatically. You'll see the main ways to set up the trigger and how to pass Excel data into the automation that follows.

steps.heading: Prepare your Excel workbook
steps.body: Store your workbook in OneDrive or SharePoint and format the relevant data as an Excel table, since table-based triggers are the most reliable way for Power Automate to detect changes.

steps.heading: Connect Excel to Power Automate
steps.body: In Power Automate, choose a trigger built around Excel Online (Business), such as one that fires when a row is added or modified in your table.

steps.heading: Configure the flow trigger
steps.body: Point the trigger at the correct file location and table, and decide whether the flow should run on new rows, edited rows, or both.

steps.heading: Pass Excel data into the flow
steps.body: Use the dynamic content from the trigger to feed row values into subsequent actions, such as sending notifications, updating records, or generating reports.

steps.heading: Test the automation with real data
steps.body: Add or edit a row in the Excel table and confirm the flow runs as expected before relying on it for business processes.

faq.question: Does the Excel file need to be in OneDrive or SharePoint?
faq.answer: Yes, the file-based Excel triggers in Power Automate require the workbook to be stored in OneDrive for Business or SharePoint rather than a local drive.

faq.question: Can I trigger a flow from a desktop Excel file?
faq.answer: Not directly from a purely local file. You would need to sync it to OneDrive or SharePoint, or use Office Scripts, for Power Automate to detect changes.

faq.question: What's the difference between using Excel tables and Office Scripts?
faq.answer: Excel tables work well with row-based triggers for simple add or update scenarios, while Office Scripts give you more control when you need custom logic run against the workbook.

faq.question: Can this automate approvals or reports?
faq.answer: Yes, once the flow is triggered by Excel data, you can chain in actions for approvals, notifications, or automated reporting based on that data.
